summary: This lesson introduces the conditional tense (would), formed by adding -ía endings to the full infinitive. It reviews the future tense and its connection to haber, and introduces the conditional with practice on bailar, ir, comer, transformar, contaminar, and imaginar, including reflexive se for passive meaning.

teachings.summary: The conditional (would) is formed by adding -ía to the full infinitive. Unlike other tenses, I and he/she/it/you(formal) share the same form (-ía). Other endings: -íamos (we), -ían (they/you guys), -ías (you informal). Like the future tense, the accent is pushed to the ending and all verb types (-ar, -er, -ir) use the same endings.
teachings.rule: Full infinitive + -ía (I/he/she/it/you formal), -ías (you informal), -íamos (we), -ían (they/you guys). The accent stays on -ía in all forms.

examples.english: I would eat, but I don't have time.

examples.spanish: Me imaginaría que no quiere ir.
examples.english: I would imagine that he doesn't want to go.

teachings.notes: Both futures (will and would) share: adding to full infinitive, stressing the ending, and grouping all verb types together.

teachings.summary: Spanish doesn't have a 'gets' construction (it gets contaminated). Instead, use 'se + verb' to express this passive meaning: 'se contamina' = it gets contaminated (literally 'it contaminates itself'). The subject isn't literally acting on itself; it's just the structure Spanish uses.
teachings.rule: Use 'se + verb' to express 'gets + past participle' in English. Se contamina = it gets contaminated. Se transformaría = it would get transformed.
teachings.notes: This does not mean the subject is literally acting on itself. It is just the Spanish structure for passive 'gets' constructions.

teachings.summary: Reviewed the future tense and its connection to haber. Practiced with bailar. Reiterated that Spanish has three ways to express future: present with context, ir a + infinitive, and the future tense (least common).

examples.english: We are going to dance later.

teachings.summary: Imaginar means to imagine. You will often hear 'me imagino' (I imagine myself) used interchangeably with 'imagino'. Adding the reflexive doesn't really change the meaning.

examples.spanish: Me imaginaría que no quiere ir.
examples.english: I would imagine that he doesn't want to go.

vocabulary.derivation: related to 'ballerina'; bailarín = dancer in Spanish
from imaginación; English j -> Spanish kh
